title: Quantum Feature Amplification Network (QFAN) as An Autoregressive Quantum Generative Model
authors: Jamal Slim, Saverio Monaco, Florian Rehm, Dirk Kruecker, Kerstin Borras
abstract: Direct-register quantum generative models for calorimeter shower simulation tie the quantum output dimension to the image dimension, so the required register size grows with the full image. Recent quantum-assisted methods reduce this pressure only by moving part of the generative task into hybrid latent-variable models. Consequently, current quantum demonstrations remain far below detector-scale geometries used in high-energy physics. We introduce the Quantum Feature Amplification Network (QFAN), which removes this register-size bottleneck by generating an image as a sequence of blocks. Each block is produced by the same small parameterized quantum circuit, conditioned on a compressed summary of the pixels already generated. Reusing the circuit fixes the qubit requirement by block size rather than full image size, while the per-step quantum processing cost is independent of image size for the Pauli-observable family used here. We derive a conservative worst-case bound on shot-noise propagation through the generation chain and give an empirical decoder-capacity heuristic for the reachable sequential depth. A three-qubit circuit with twelve shared variational parameters, closed-form ridge decoders, and a post-hoc residual sampler reproduces per-pixel intensity distributions, inter-pixel correlations, and total energy distributions of calorimeter showers on both simulator and IBM quantum hardware. At this scale, the hardware-simulator gap is consistent with optimization-budget limits dominating over device noise, although the experiments do not causally separate these effects. The results establish a hardware-compatible proof of principle and motivate, but do not validate, larger-scale extrapolations within this circuit family.
summary: This paper introduces the Quantum Feature Amplification Network (QFAN), an autoregressive quantum generative model designed to simulate large-scale images like calorimeter showers. By generating the image sequentially in fixed-size blocks, QFAN bypasses the prohibitive requirement that quantum register size must grow with the image size. The authors demonstrate this feasibility by reproducing key physical observables on both simulators and real quantum hardware.
main_problem: To develop quantum generative models capable of simulating large-scale physical images, such as calorimeter showers, without requiring the quantum register size to scale linearly with the image dimension.
main_result: The Quantum Feature Amplification Network (QFAN) successfully establishes a hardware-compatible proof of principle by generating images block-by-block, keeping the required qubit count fixed regardless of the total image size.
method: The model uses an autoregressive factorization, where each block is generated by reusing a small parameterized quantum circuit conditioned on a fixed-length summary (count-sketch) of previously generated pixels.
model_or_system: The system simulates electromagnetic calorimeter showers, treating the energy deposition map as a high-dimensional image ($\mathbf{y}$). The model architecture is QFAN, which decomposes the image into sequential blocks ($B$) and uses a shared quantum circuit ($U(\mathbf{a}_ eta, 	heta)$) for generation.
key_observables: Per-pixel intensity distributions, inter-pixel correlation matrices, and the total energy distribution of the simulated showers.
important_parameters: Qubit count ($n_q=3$), block size ($b$), total number of blocks ($B$), and the feature-to-target ratio ($ho$).
main_assumptions: The training uses teacher-forcing (ground-truth prefixes), and the analysis cannot causally separate optimization-budget limitations from device noise effects.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison between direct-register scaling ($n_q=d$) and QFAN's fixed-qubit approach; subsequent figures show the successful reproduction of marginal distributions, correlation structures, and total energy conservation across simulated and hardware backends.
paper_structure: The paper introduces QFAN to solve the scaling bottleneck, details the autoregressive block generation process involving count-sketch conditioning and feature extraction, presents theoretical bounds on noise propagation, and validates the approach by reproducing key physical observables on both simulators and IBM quantum hardware.
why_interesting: The core mechanism—decoupling resource scaling from output dimension via autoregression and fixed-size quantum circuits—is a major architectural advance for applying quantum computation to high-dimensional physical simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Paradigm comparison. Prior direct-register QML requires nq=d qubits (left). QFAN reuses a fixed nq-qubit circuit across B blocks (right), so qubit requirements are de- termined by block size rather than by the full image dimen- sion.

FIG. 2. Compression–expansion view of QFAN. At autore- gressive step β, a fixed-length sketch compresses the previ- ously generated prefix and is projected to circuit angles. The same shared parameterized quantum circuit is reused across all B blocks; its measurements produce Pauli features, which are decoded into the next block output ˆyβ. Repeating this cycle and feeding each generated block back into the sketch update yields the assembled image ˜y ∈Rd.

FIG. 4. Per-pixel marginal intensity distributions (variable intensity scale). MC truth (blue), IBM hardware (red), and simulator (green) are shown for each pixel, with MC/QFAN ratio panels below. Peak position shifts systematically from low intensity (pixel 0) to high intensity (pixel 5), reflecting longitudinal shower development. Both QFAN paths reproduce the MC distributions across all pixels.

FIG. 5. Pearson correlation matrices (12×12). Top row: MC truth (a), simulator QFAN (b), and IBM hardware QFAN (c). Bottom row: pairwise differences. The MC−SIM (d) and MC−IBM (e) residuals are similar in magnitude and concentrated at the block boundary (pixels 4–7). The IBM−SIM difference (f) is the smallest, indicating that the two QFAN paths agree more closely with each other than either does with MC.
